author: raoulritter

body:
The `af-magic` theme uses `%~` to show the current directory while substituting your home directory with `~`. You can tell zsh how many path segments to show by putting a number between `%` and `~`: `%1~` will only show 1 path segment (the current directory name).

If that's what you want, you can modify this line https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/blob/c172624398918577bcb212d07b0036f4058cb5c6/themes/af-magic.zsh-theme#L22
and replace `%~` for `%1~`.

Note that first you have to set it up as a custom theme. See [the guide here](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/wiki/Customization#overriding-and-adding-themes).

----
If you instead want to shrink the previous path segments you could take a look at the [`shrink-path` plugin](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/blob/master/plugins/shrink-path).

```sh
# Get user's update preferences
#
# Supported update modes:

# Cancel update if:

|| [[ ! -w "$ZSH" || ! -O "$ZSH" ]] \
|| ! command -v git &>/dev/null; then
  unset update_mode
  return
fi
+/home/user/.oh-my-zsh/tools/check_for_upgrade.sh:23> [[ disabled = disabled ]]
+/home/user/.oh-my-zsh/tools/check_for_upgrade.sh:26> unset update_mode

body:
On this trace the update is disabled correctly. You can try to trace only `check_for_upgrade.sh` so you can see what happens on an ssh connection where it does update. The file you should patch is at `$ZSH/tools/check_for_upgrade.sh`:
```patch
diff --git a/tools/check_for_upgrade.sh b/tools/check_for_upgrade.sh
index 293f48ed..40ab3010 100644
--- a/tools/check_for_upgrade.sh
+++ b/tools/check_for_upgrade.sh
@@ -3,6 +3,7 @@ if [[ -f ~/.zsh-update && ! -f "${ZSH_CACHE_DIR}/.zsh-update" ]]; then
   mv ~/.zsh-update "${ZSH_CACHE_DIR}/.zsh-update"
 fi
 
+set -xv
 # Get user's update preferences
 #
 # Supported update modes:
@@ -24,8 +25,10 @@ if [[ "$update_mode" = disabled ]] \
    || [[ ! -w "$ZSH" || ! -O "$ZSH" ]] \
    || ! command -v git &>/dev/null; then
   unset update_mode
+  echo cancelled && set +xv
   return
 fi
+echo continued && set +xv
 
 function current_epoch() {
   zmodload zsh/datetime
```
After that, login again and see what happens when it updates.

body:
It looks like you set `DISABLE_UPDATE_PROMPT`, which sets the update mode to `auto`:
```sh
# Support old-style settings
[[ "$DISABLE_UPDATE_PROMPT" != true ]] || update_mode=auto
+/home/user/.oh-my-zsh/tools/check_for_upgrade.sh:17> [[ true != true ]]
+/home/user/.oh-my-zsh/tools/check_for_upgrade.sh:17> update_mode=auto
```
That setting means *update without asking*. With the confusion you might have wanted to set `DISABLE_AUTO_UPDATE`.

This setting takes precedence over the `zstyle` one, though it being older it should be ignored if `zstyle` is set (that is a bug).

In any case, you should get rid of that setting, as it is deprecated, and just use the `zstyle` `mode disabled` setting.

body:
In my opinion, the best UX is to just show a notification of new updates (like what homebrew is using), users who want to update can use `omz update` manually.

Showing a prompt while bash starts is annoying:
- Automation tools (like run test-cases, run debug,... in vscode) suddenly fail when encountering the prompt.
- Fast command typing operations (for me) are affected.
author: dphans

body: Hey that's an option, just use `mode reminder` https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh?tab=readme-ov-file#getting-updates
author: mcornella

body: Please do this!  The prompt is painful for automation and a potential security hole anyway.  Suppose your code gets compromised!
author: bkusenda

body:
I'm more worried about automatic security patches not getting installed than about a compromise of our repository. We have extensive system and personal security controls for it.

But if we can bypass update settings when there's a security advisory (in our roadmap to be implemented soon), we could make the default a reminder.

That said, we already have logic to bypass the update prompt if any characters where written to input before the prompt shows up, it seems it doesn't work for all cases, but it'd be good to have bug reports for that to make it better.

body:
What do you mean? If you want to define other aliases and put them in another file, you can use a custom file that you put in `$ZSH_CUSTOM/<name>.zsh` (e.g. `$ZSH_CUSTOM/aliases.zsh`) and that will automatically get loaded by Oh My Zsh.

Another option is to put them in any file you want and then `source` it from your zshrc file (make sure to put the `source` line after OMZ is sourced).

body:
It's probable that the line defining plugins is written below the line sourcing OMZ. Check your zshrc file for the correct order:

## ❌ This does not work

```zsh
...
source $ZSH/oh-my-zsh.sh
...
plugins=(enabled plugins go here)
```

## ✅ It should look like this

```zsh
...
plugins=(enabled plugins go here)
...
source $ZSH/oh-my-zsh.sh
```

You could leverage `zle-line-init` to set the keymap to `vicmd`. This would work without overriding previous zle-line-init hooks:
```zsh
autoload -Uz add-zle-hook-widget
add-zle-hook-widget line-init vi-cmd-mode
```

body: The docs say the pager is used by default in awscli v2. If `$PAGER` is set, the output is paged. You can turns this off with `cli_pager=` in a profile or setting `AWS_PAGER=""`: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cli/latest/userguide/cli-usage-pagination.html#cli-usage-pagination-clientside.

body:
Since they both use the same variable to check it's impossible to target one or the other. Since `$SHOW_AWS_PROMPT` is only read on plugin load by the `aws` plugin you could set it before OMZ is sourced and then unset it after OMZ is sourced. That way you'd see the prompt defined by the agnoster theme.

Another option is to override the `aws_prompt_info` with an empty body: 
```sh
aws_prompt_info() {}
```
or remove it from `$RPROMPT` manually:
```sh
RPROMPT="${RPROMPT//\$\(aws_prompt_info\)}"
```

author: mcornella

body:
> Can I override `omz_termsupport_preexec` somehow to stop that behavior?

It seems that I can! I just have to do it AFTER the full OMZ script is sourced:

```sh
source $ZSH/oh-my-zsh.sh

# override setting command name as tab title
function omz_termsupport_preexec {
  return
}
```
author: Jean85

body:
When you change completions folder by yourself (not omz updates) you'll need to delete the following file: `~/.zcompdump-<hostname>-<zsh-version`. This is bound to change in following updates (#10672), but for the moment you have to do so.
Tell me if this workaround works for you ;)
author: carlosala

body:
Hm, "...change completions folder...". Not sure I've done that intentionally 😅. What is the recommended way of installing completions (for example for `kind`)?

Looks like deleting `~/.zcompdump-<hostname>-<zsh-version` works though. Thanks! 👍
author: jooooel

body:
When saying change completions folder I meant change content of that directory.
For the second question, you can have a directory wherever you want to have it containing all your custom completions and just add the following snippet in your `.zshrc` (before sourcing `.oh-my-zsh.sh`):
```zsh
fpath=("/path/to/that/directory" $fpath)
```

```

<details>
<summary> eksctl completion </summary>

```
#compdef anywhere

# zsh completion for anywhere                             -*- shell-script -*-

__anywhere_debug()
{
    local file="$BASH_COMP_DEBUG_FILE"
    if [[ -n ${file} ]]; then
        echo "$*" >> "${file}"
    fi
}

_anywhere()
{
    local shellCompDirectiveError=1
    local shellCompDirectiveNoSpace=2
    local shellCompDirectiveNoFileComp=4
    local shellCompDirectiveFilterFileExt=8
    local shellCompDirectiveFilterDirs=16

    local lastParam lastChar flagPrefix requestComp out directive comp lastComp noSpace
    local -a completions

    __anywhere_debug "\n========= starting completion logic =========="
    __anywhere_debug "CURRENT: ${CURRENT}, words[*]: ${words[*]}"

    # The user could have moved the cursor backwards on the command-line.
    # We need to trigger completion from the $CURRENT location, so we need
    # to truncate the command-line ($words) up to the $CURRENT location.
    # (We cannot use $CURSOR as its value does not work when a command is an alias.)
    words=("${=words[1,CURRENT]}")
    __anywhere_debug "Truncated words[*]: ${words[*]},"

    lastParam=${words[-1]}
    lastChar=${lastParam[-1]}
    __anywhere_debug "lastParam: ${lastParam}, lastChar: ${lastChar}"

    # For zsh, when completing a flag with an = (e.g., anywhere -n=<TAB>)
    # completions must be prefixed with the flag
    setopt local_options BASH_REMATCH
    if [[ "${lastParam}" =~ '-.*=' ]]; then
        # We are dealing with a flag with an =
        flagPrefix="-P ${BASH_REMATCH}"
    fi

    # Prepare the command to obtain completions
    requestComp="${words[1]} __complete ${words[2,-1]}"
    if [ "${lastChar}" = "" ]; then
        # If the last parameter is complete (there is a space following it)
        # We add an extra empty parameter so we can indicate this to the go completion code.
        __anywhere_debug "Adding extra empty parameter"
        requestComp="${requestComp} \"\""
    fi

    __anywhere_debug "About to call: eval ${requestComp}"

    # Use eval to handle any environment variables and such
    out=$(eval ${requestComp} 2>/dev/null)
    __anywhere_debug "completion output: ${out}"

    # Extract the directive integer following a : from the last line
    local lastLine
    while IFS='\n' read -r line; do
        lastLine=${line}
    done < <(printf "%s\n" "${out[@]}")
    __anywhere_debug "last line: ${lastLine}"

    if [ "${lastLine[1]}" = : ]; then
        directive=${lastLine[2,-1]}
        # Remove the directive including the : and the newline
        local suffix
        (( suffix=${#lastLine}+2))
        out=${out[1,-$suffix]}
    else
        # There is no directive specified.  Leave $out as is.
        __anywhere_debug "No directive found.  Setting do default"
        directive=0
    fi

    __anywhere_debug "directive: ${directive}"
    __anywhere_debug "completions: ${out}"
    __anywhere_debug "flagPrefix: ${flagPrefix}"

    if [ $((directive & shellCompDirectiveError)) -ne 0 ]; then
        __anywhere_debug "Completion received error. Ignoring completions."
        return
    fi

    local activeHelpMarker="_activeHelp_ "
    local endIndex=${#activeHelpMarker}
    local startIndex=$((${#activeHelpMarker}+1))
    local hasActiveHelp=0
    while IFS='\n' read -r comp; do
        # Check if this is an activeHelp statement (i.e., prefixed with $activeHelpMarker)
        if [ "${comp[1,$endIndex]}" = "$activeHelpMarker" ];then
            __anywhere_debug "ActiveHelp found: $comp"
            comp="${comp[$startIndex,-1]}"
            if [ -n "$comp" ]; then
                compadd -x "${comp}"
                __anywhere_debug "ActiveHelp will need delimiter"
                hasActiveHelp=1
            fi

            continue
        fi

        if [ -n "$comp" ]; then
            # If requested, completions are returned with a description.
            # The description is preceded by a TAB character.
            # For zsh's _describe, we need to use a : instead of a TAB.
            # We first need to escape any : as part of the completion itself.
            comp=${comp//:/\\:}

            local tab="$(printf '\t')"
            comp=${comp//$tab/:}

            __anywhere_debug "Adding completion: ${comp}"
            completions+=${comp}
            lastComp=$comp
        fi
    done < <(printf "%s\n" "${out[@]}")

    # Add a delimiter after the activeHelp statements, but only if:
    # - there are completions following the activeHelp statements, or
    # - file completion will be performed (so there will be choices after the activeHelp)
    if [ $hasActiveHelp -eq 1 ]; then
        if [ ${#completions} -ne 0 ] || [ $((directive & shellCompDirectiveNoFileComp)) -eq 0 ]; then
            __anywhere_debug "Adding activeHelp delimiter"
            compadd -x "--"
            hasActiveHelp=0
        fi
    fi

    if [ $((directive & shellCompDirectiveNoSpace)) -ne 0 ]; then
        __anywhere_debug "Activating nospace."
        noSpace="-S ''"
    fi

    if [ $((directive & shellCompDirectiveFilterFileExt)) -ne 0 ]; then
        # File extension filtering
        local filteringCmd
        filteringCmd='_files'
        for filter in ${completions[@]}; do
            if [ ${filter[1]} != '*' ]; then
                # zsh requires a glob pattern to do file filtering
                filter="\*.$filter"
            fi
            filteringCmd+=" -g $filter"
        done
        filteringCmd+=" ${flagPrefix}"

        __anywhere_debug "File filtering command: $filteringCmd"
        _arguments '*:filename:'"$filteringCmd"
    elif [ $((directive & shellCompDirectiveFilterDirs)) -ne 0 ]; then
        # File completion for directories only
        local subdir
        subdir="${completions[1]}"
        if [ -n "$subdir" ]; then
            __anywhere_debug "Listing directories in $subdir"
            pushd "${subdir}" >/dev/null 2>&1
        else
            __anywhere_debug "Listing directories in ."
        fi

        local result
        _arguments '*:dirname:_files -/'" ${flagPrefix}"
        result=$?
        if [ -n "$subdir" ]; then
            popd >/dev/null 2>&1
        fi
        return $result
    else
        __anywhere_debug "Calling _describe"
        if eval _describe "completions" completions $flagPrefix $noSpace; then
            __anywhere_debug "_describe found some completions"

            # Return the success of having called _describe
            return 0
        else
            __anywhere_debug "_describe did not find completions."
            __anywhere_debug "Checking if we should do file completion."
            if [ $((directive & shellCompDirectiveNoFileComp)) -ne 0 ]; then
                __anywhere_debug "deactivating file completion"

                # We must return an error code here to let zsh know that there were no
                # completions found by _describe; this is what will trigger other
                # matching algorithms to attempt to find completions.
                # For example zsh can match letters in the middle of words.
                return 1
            else
                # Perform file completion
                __anywhere_debug "Activating file completion"

                # We must return the result of this command, so it must be the
                # last command, or else we must store its result to return it.
                _arguments '*:filename:_files'" ${flagPrefix}"
            fi
        fi
    fi
}

# don't run the completion function when being source-ed or eval-ed
if [ "$funcstack[1]" = "_anywhere" ]; then
    _anywhere
fi
```
</details>

```sh
ZSH_THEME_GIT_PROMPT_DIRTY="%{$fg[red]%}●%{$fg[yellow]%}"

author: rwmitchell

body:
```sh
# reload zsrhrc
exec zsh

# create the virtualenv
mkvirtualenv --python=python3.7.5 -a `pwd` hn
```

After `exec zsh` the rest of the script stops running, since you've changed processes. The one that was reading the script is no longer running. You should figure out a way to run that in the zsh process.
author: mcornella

body: is there a way to load the virtualenvwrapper plugin in this process?
author: r33drichards

body:
Since the script is running in zsh, you should be able to load `virtualenvwrapper` before making the call to `mkvirtualenv` by loading the `virtualenvwrapper` plugin if you know the value of `$ZSH`:

```sh
# load virtualenvwrapper
source "$ZSH/plugins/virtualenvwrapper/virtualenvwrapper.plugin.zsh"
# create the virtualenv
mkvirtualenv --python=python3.7.5 -a `pwd` hn

# reload zsh
exec zsh
```

An alternative is to [manually load virtualenvwrapper as per their documentation](https://virtualenvwrapper.readthedocs.io/en/latest/install.html#shell-startup-file) and then run `mkvirtualenv`.

Another option is to run the `mkvirutalenv` command in interactive zsh to force it to read the zshrc file, which will load OMZ and therefore the virtualenvwrapper plugin:

```sh
zsh -ic 'mkvirtualenv --python=python3.7.5 -a `pwd` hn'
```

#!/usr/bin/env zsh

brew install pyenv
echo 'export PYENV_ROOT="$HOME/.pyenv"' >> ~/.zshrc
echo 'eval "$(pyenv init -)"' >> ~/.zshrc
export PYENV_ROOT="$HOME/.pyenv"
export PATH="$PYENV_ROOT/bin:$PATH"    # if `pyenv` is not already on PATH
eval "$(pyenv init --path)"
eval "$(pyenv init -)"
pyenv install -s 3.7.5

sudo apt update && sudo apt install -y build-essential python3.8-venv python3-pip python3-distutils python3-apt
echo "export PATH=/home/brev/.local/bin:\$PATH" >> ~/.zshrc
export PATH=/home/brev/.local/bin:$PATH
pip3 install virtualenv
pip3 install virtualenvwrapper

sed -e '/plugins/ s/^#*/#/' -i ~/.zshrc
sed -e '/source/ s/^#*/#/' -i ~/.zshrc
echo "plugins=(git virtualenvwrapper)" >> ~/.zshrc
echo "source \$ZSH/oh-my-zsh.sh" >> ~/.zshrc
zsh -ic 'mkvirtualenv --python=3.7.5 -a /home/brev/workspace/hn hn'
echo -n "created virtual env"

```

author: mcornella

body:
The aliases plugin automatically groups aliases by the command, or else it puts them in the `_default` group if there are no other aliases for the same command. It's not made to customize the output, but a hacky way to do that is to define an alias that does nothing (`alias custom_group=''`) and use that as the first part of your aliases:

```sh
alias juanitoddd=''
alias alias1='juanitoddd ls -l'
alias alias2='juanitoddd cd /root'
alias alias3='juanitoddd pwd'
```

body:
The `af-magic` theme does not use any bold fonts. If what you mean is how to remove bold output for the `ls` command, you need to modify either [`$LSCOLORS` or `$LS_COLORS`](https://gist.github.com/thomd/7667642) depending on your `ls` version.

Post the output of `ls --version` and `typeset -p LSCOLORS LS_COLORS` so I can guide you towards removing bold formatting for `ls`.

The similar issues you posted also mention globally-disabling bold text. I assume that's not what you need and you only need to disable bold formatting in `ls` output.
author: mcornella

body:
export LS_COLORS=${LS_COLORS}'di=00;34:ln=00;36'

solve the problem, thank you very much!

body:
Put this in your zshrc file:
```sh
bindkey -M viins 'jl' vi-cmd-mode
```
Note that for multi-char keybindings to work, you need to press both faster than `$KEYTIMEOUT` milliseconds. You can change this variable to have more or less time between key presses.

body:
There already was a `.zshrc` file and it was kept (see the output from the installation):
``` 
Looking for an existing zsh config...
Found ~/.zshrc. Keeping
```
This means that the installer was run with `--keep-zshrc` or `KEEP_ZSHRC=yes`. Because of that, the default OMZ loading lines aren't in your zshrc file, so OMZ is not actually loaded.

This is the minimum loading lines to load OMZ that you should fill out and put in your zshrc file:
```sh
export ZSH=<path/to/ohmyzsh>
ZSH_THEME=<theme>
plugins=(<plugins> <to> <load>)
source $ZSH/oh-my-zsh.sh
```

body: Nevermind, I remember we default the `emacs` command to `emacsclient --no-wait`, which wants to open a graphical terminal. To use `-nw` you have to use the `te` alias, which doesn't have the `--no-wait` flag. I could modify the plugin to remove the `--no-wait` flag if `-nw` is passed though.

body:
probably the easiest thing is to edit the theme itself, adding something like that at the top of your zshrc:
```
[[ $TERM == screen ]] && SCREEN_PREFIX="<whatever you want>
```
And then in the PROMPT variables of the theme adding `$SCREEN_PREFIX` where you want to print that prefix.
With pl10k is actually really difficult to understand where are things happening, but you can still try. I don't know about it, maybe there's some config that you can use, check their repo for confirmation.

body:
The `frontcube` theme adds an empty line to the PROMPT by design. You can follow [this guide](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/wiki/Customization#overriding-and-adding-themes) to copy the theme to the [`$ZSH_CUSTOM` folder](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/wiki/Settings#zsh_custom) and remove the newline from the prompt, so https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/blob/d41ca84af1271e8bfbe26f581cebe3b86521d0db/themes/frontcube.zsh-theme#L2-L4
would look like
```sh
PROMPT='$fg_bold[gray]%}%~%{$fg_bold[blue]%}%{$fg_bold[blue]%} % %{$reset_color%} 
%{$fg[green]%}➞  %{$reset_color%'
```
author: mcornella

body:
I tried this on a test container and it looks like there will be a small error at first that you can make go away by running `zsh` again.

Just in case something goes wrong though, be sure to run a new `zsh` session (do not use `exec zsh`), and only when `echo $SHLVL` shows 2 or more, run the upgrade. This is only to make sure that there's no error that prevents loading a working shell session.

Another thing that would work is having multiple tabs open and only perform the update there. After the update, open a new tab, and if everything seems to work correctly then that's all well and done.

There may be some changes in plugins or themes that you use but none that will prevent loading a shell session. Reply to this answer with a list of in-use plugins and theme if you need more details about this.

And as always, if you have an error open a new issue with the details (again, if it's an error that prevents open a new shell, do not close any tabs open and contact again).

body:
If you want to copy the latest command that you executed, for example, you can use:
```sh
echo "!!" | clipcopy
```
Note that this is only going to work if you are using `ohmyzsh`, as `clipcopy` is defined there 👌🏻 

You can also go to `~/.zsh_history` file to copy other commands that you executed previously.

body:
Hi, this means that Oh My Zsh was loaded from a non-zsh process. In this case, the process tree indicates that it was loaded as part of the last command:
```
/bin/sh .git/hooks/post-checkout
```
You should inspect the `post-checkout` hook to understand why it was loaded. This could be a `source` line in the `post-checkout` file, or even that you are `source`-ing OMZ inside the `.profile` file.

author: lokize

body:
I don't know if this helps for future issues but I just ran into this and I found the adding a couple values to my .zshrc file and removing a directory history file did it for me. 

```.zshrc
# Add history-substring-search plugin (this was essential)
plugins=(history-substring-search)

# History configuration - after Oh My Zsh is sourced (possibly optional)
HISTFILE=~/.zsh_history
HISTSIZE=50000
SAVEHIST=50000

# History options (possibly optional)
setopt EXTENDED_HISTORY       # record timestamp of command in HISTFILE
setopt HIST_EXPIRE_DUPS_FIRST # delete duplicates first when HISTFILE size exceeds HISTSIZE
setopt HIST_IGNORE_DUPS       # ignore duplicated commands history list
setopt HIST_IGNORE_SPACE      # ignore commands that start with space
setopt HIST_VERIFY            # show command with history expansion to user before running it
setopt SHARE_HISTORY          # share command history data
setopt APPEND_HISTORY         # append to history file immediately

# Initialize history (this was essential)
fc -p "$HISTFILE"
```

Then I ran `rm -rf ~/.directory_history` and then reopened my terminal.

body:
@rilysh is spot on. To install zsh there are two paths that I'd suggest as Unraid doesn't come with a package manager.

Option 1. 
Follow the steps provided [here](https://forums.unraid.net/topic/35866-unraid-6-nerdpack-cli-tools-iftop-iotop-screen-kbd-etc/?do=findComment&comment=1159381) which state:
```
To replicate the functionality (unsupported):
- Go to https://slackware.pkgs.org/15.0/slackware-x86_64/ which 
lists packages for Slackware 15 Unraid is based on
Search for the packages you want
- Download the txz files for them, and put them on the flash drive 
in /extra (/boot/extra on a running system), that will cause them to 
auto-install on boot (create the folder if there isn't one)
- To be able to use them without a reboot use unraid CLI to navigate 
where you put the packages and run installpkg <filename>
```
Option 2. 
Slightly more advanced option would be to manually install @ich777's un-get plugin available [here](https://github.com/ich777/un-get). This is more advanced but if you install packages regularly, will make things a lot easier. 

Note that while installing a package such as zsh is unlikely to cause any problems, it's not supported by Lime, and there are a lot of plugins available via slackware that will irreparably damage your Unraid installation: Unraid isn't a standard Linux distro, and relies on a lot of proprietary customisation. Please ensure you back up your flash drive first, and be careful what you install.

Best to get support for this issue over on the [Unraid forums](https://forums.unraid.net/) though.

body:
Can you specify exactly what the command would do? `find` is used to search for files, while `grep` is used to find a specific string in the file contents (`cat` is not needed here: `grep string file`).

How would you combine both? If you are talking about something like, *find hello in all files ending with .txt*, you could use wildcards directly:

```sh
grep hello *.txt
```
author: mcornella

body:
The current analysis for the install.sh URL shows only 1 currently, as I rerun the analysis. The previous results were from an analysis from 2022.

In any case, these results are false positives, as there is no possible malware coming from a url that serves a plain text file.

The proper analysis should be done on the actual script file, which you can find [here](https://www.virustotal.com/gui/file/73bc685bfac98e4d41c41d49b2d4d7bbcf55cbac7a3dbb4022073410ff15fd03/summary). You'll see that 0 positives are returned.

You can also inspect the install.sh, which is very straightforward, it only runs git and sets up zsh.

Let me know if you have any other doubts, and thanks for reporting this.

author: robbyrussell

body:
If you've got something that _you_ find useful, open a PR.

I'm a FreeBSD user but really can't find much in my configuration that is especially FreeBSD specific. Stuff like `freebsd-update fetch install` could perhaps be aliased.

From a quick glance at the archlinux plugin, it looks mostly like pacman aliases, often prefixing them with `sudo`. Given that FreeBSD doesn't include `sudo` in base and many FreeBSD people either use `doas` instead or prefer plain `su` I'm not sure how useful that would be. Note also that `pkg` supports it's own aliases - see the ALIAS block in `/usr/local/etc/pkg.conf`. The macos plugin seems to be functions reflecting areas where macos differs from other Unix systems.

In my opinion, even for ArchLinux, it should test `${+commands[sudo]}` and `(( EUID ))` before including `sudo` in the alias.

Given your test, it doesn't seem to be an issue with `OMZ`.

You could try to figure out who is calling `zsh` twice by echoing the parent process id at the top of `.zshrc`:    `echo $PPID`.

nihil     103684  0.8  0.3 1859044 122516 ?      Sl   11:06   0:01 konsole

body:
You can define your own zle widget that calls `tput reset`, which clears everything in the terminal:

```zsh
clear-terminal() { tput reset; zle redisplay; }
zle -N clear-terminal
bindkey '^L' clear-terminal
```

body:
For anybody with the same issue: starting tmux with `u` flag solves this issue for me.

```sh
tmux -u
```

> [-u](http://man.openbsd.org/OpenBSD-current/man1/tmux.1#u)      Write UTF-8 output to the terminal even if the first environment variable of LC_ALL, LC_CTYPE, or LANG that is set does not contain "UTF-8" or "UTF8"

Apparently `utf8` was causing issues, see:
- https://superuser.com/questions/651868/weird-behaviour-rendering-on-tmux-in-osx

You can run the following command:
`sh ~/.oh-my-zsh/tools/uninstall.sh`

You will be asked the following:

author: JimBimBum

body:
I tried every solution here, including uninstalling, and nothing worked. For anyone who doesn't want to remove Oh My Zsh but wants to kill the compinit error for good, this is the only thing that fixed it for me.

### What Didn't Work

- Running `chmod` on any directory.
- Deleting the `.zcompdump` file.
- Adding `ZSH_DISABLE_COMPFIX=true` to my `~/.zshrc`. The error still appeared.

### What Worked

The problem is another file loads before `~/.zshrc`, so the fix is too late. You have to put the command in the file that loads first: `~/.zshenv`.

Run this one command in your terminal:

`echo 'ZSH_DISABLE_COMPFIX=true' >> ~/.zshenv`

Restart your terminal. The error is gone.

body:
Hey @asrmarco13 I had the same issue, tracked it down to being the p10k instant prompt setting.  I commented it out as such, 


```
# Enable Powerlevel10k instant prompt. Should stay close to the top of ~/.zshrc.
# Initialization code that may require console input (password prompts, [y/n]
# confirmations, etc.) must go above this block; everything else may go below.
#if [[ -r "${XDG_CACHE_HOME:-$HOME/.cache}/p10k-instant-prompt-${(%):-%n}.zsh" ]]; then
#  source "${XDG_CACHE_HOME:-$HOME/.cache}/p10k-instant-prompt-${(%):-%n}.zsh"
#fi
```


If you don't want to fully comment it out, just move the fast fetch code above it.  so that it will look like this. 


```
if [[ -o interactive ]]; then
    fastfetch --load-config dr460nized
fi

# Enable Powerlevel10k instant prompt. Should stay close to the top of ~/.zshrc.
# Initialization code that may require console input (password prompts, [y/n]
# confirmations, etc.) must go above this block; everything else may go below.
if [[ -r "${XDG_CACHE_HOME:-$HOME/.cache}/p10k-instant-prompt-${(%):-%n}.zsh" ]]; then
  source "${XDG_CACHE_HOME:-$HOME/.cache}/p10k-instant-prompt-${(%):-%n}.zsh"
fi
```

body:
Hello I found the solution to my need. 

Here is the solution i used the generic colouriser of garabik : https://github.com/garabik/grc 

You need to install it and then create inside .grc a file custom_grc.conf and paste this : 

```
regexp=DBG
colour=green
-
regexp=.*INF.*
colour=blue
-
regexp=.*WRN.*
colour=yellow
-
regexp=.*ERR.*
colour=red

```
Then you can use the command 

```sh
mycommand | grcat custom_grc.conf
```

fi

author: arielcorte

body:
It looks like the part that fails is the first line in `.zshrc`:

```sh
﻿# If you come from bash you might have to change your $PATH.
+/home/user/.zshrc:1> '﻿#' If you come from bash you might have to change your /home/user/.cargo/bin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/local/sbin:/usr/bin:/usr/sbin:/var/lib/snapd/snap/bin.
+command_not_found_handle:1> local runcnf=1
+command_not_found_handle:2> local retval=127
+command_not_found_handle:5> [[ 569Xivx == *i* ]]
+command_not_found_handle:8> [[ ! -S /run/dbus/system_bus_socket ]]
+command_not_found_handle:11> [[ ! -x /usr/libexec/packagekitd ]]
+command_not_found_handle:14> [[ -n '' ]]
+command_not_found_handle:17> [[ ! -x /usr/libexec/pk-command-not-found ]]
+command_not_found_handle:20> [ 1 -eq 1 ']'
+command_not_found_handle:21> /usr/libexec/pk-command-not-found '﻿#' If you come from bash you might have to change your /home/user/.cargo/bin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/local/sbin:/usr/bin:/usr/sbin:/var/lib/snapd/snap/bin.

zsh: ﻿#: command not found...
```

It is interpreting the `#` as a command, and not as a comment. This is not happening with the rest of comments, so my guess is that there is a character before or after making it different than a regular # character.

You can use `hexdump -C .zshrc | head -5` to confirm the binary code of the first 5 lines. Either way, the solution might be just to delete the first lines and recreate them.

+command_not_found_handle:14> [[ -n '' ]]
+command_not_found_handle:17> [[ ! -x /usr/libexec/pk-command-not-found ]]
+command_not_found_handle:20> [ 1 -eq 1 ']'
+command_not_found_handle:21> /usr/libexec/pk-command-not-found '<feff>#' If you come from bash you might have to change your /home/arielcorte/.cargo/bin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/local/sbin:/usr/bin:/usr/sbin:/var/lib/snapd/snap/bin.
zsh: <feff>#: command not found...
+command_not_found_handle:22> retval=127 
+command_not_found_handle:28> return 127
# export PATH=$HOME/bin:/usr/local/bin:$PATH
```
Note: I have manually added the \<feff> in my comment, but in my file there was the actual character.

then I went to both my .bashrc and .zshrc files and with vim I used `:set nobomb` (actually it only changed the .zshrc file, but I tested both just in case) and it was fixed :)

Thanks @carlosala for the log snippet and help and @mcornella for the hint for looking for `#:` (I find it in my logs).

This is what I mean - every linux terminal comes with the function [ctrl +r] to cycle through your commands history. author: tmel173

body: This resolved my issue btw but I wanted to understand further. Thanks very much!
author: tmel173

body:
@tmel173 of course, no problem! By default, zsh does not have the `^r` shortcut for reverse history search (you can reproduce it by executing `zsh -f` and trying!). We add it [here](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/blob/bbda81fe4b338f00bbf7c7f33e6d1b12d067dc05/lib/key-bindings.zsh#L109), but if you run `bindkey -v` after loading `oh-my-zsh.sh` you override all key-bindings. You can reproduce this by executing in a clean ohmyzsh installation (with no `bindkey` calls):
```zsh
bindkey | grep '\^Xr' # you'll see an entry
bindkey -v # set viins keymap
bindkey | grep '\^Xr' # no entry anymore
```
By adding the `vi-mode` plugin, you get the `bindkey -v` executed but maintain all the good stuff like the `^r` keymap.
I hope this explanation fulfils your doubts!
author: carlosala

body: That explains why other shortcut keys were also broken ! Thanks a lot for the explanation !

body: more importantly, OMZ sets bindkey to emacs mode in the startup code, overriding anything you may have already set.  vi-mode sets it back to vi.  zsh already defaults to emacs mode, OMZ should not be setting it in the startup.

body:
You would need to add the `$RPROMPT` contents to your `$PROMPT` on the top line, and add a spacer string (of empty spaces) so that the content is displayed on the right. As far as I know there's no other way around that:

```
PROMPT="%n@%m %~<a bunch of spaces>%T"$'\n> '
```

The spacer can be computed dynamically from the content you display and the amount of characters per row. You can check this very thorough answer: https://unix.stackexchange.com/a/250457

Hello @volga629-1, thanks for the report.

We take extremist views on our project very seriously and we use moderation to ensure that we don't give a platform to them.

Fortunately in this case, the symbols in the external theme documented in our wiki [1] belong to a religion.

Swastikas have long been a symbol of good fortune in several Asian religions [2], many hundreds (and possibly thousands) of years before nazism co-opted them. Unfortunately the symbol is now most known for nazism, but it is still very widely used among countries professing such religions [2].

I have added a disclaimer in [1] for future reference. 

[1] https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/wiki/External-themes#amresh-terminal-theme

[2] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Swastika

I added this to my .zshrc file as a temporary fix. It looks similar but I liked the old look more.

```
function parse_git_branch() {

COLOR_DEF=$'%f'
COLOR_DIR=$'%F{39}'
COLOR_GIT=$'%F{197}'
setopt PROMPT_SUBST
export PROMPT='${COLOR_DIR}%1~ ${COLOR_GIT}$(parse_git_branch)${COLOR_DEF} '
```

author: ChaseTheWebDev

body:
Hi Chase! If the content of `.zprofile` is a literal copy-paste, the `PATH` variable is missing a dollar, otherwise the previous contents are not preserved. Here's how it should look:

```sh
# double quotes added for good measure, in case there are spaces in some of the paths
export PATH="${PATH}:/usr/local/mysql-8.0.36-macos14-x86_64/bin"
```

As to code formatting in GitHub, it uses Markdown, so you can use [code blocks](https://docs.github.com/en/get-started/writing-on-github/getting-started-with-writing-and-formatting-on-github/basic-writing-and-formatting-syntax#quoting-code) so that code is shown as is (and even with syntax highlighting).

body:
It looks to me like `code` thinks it's running under macOS and running `open -a "Visual Studio Code"`, which is used to open VS Code within macOS.

I don't see we are doing anything like that within Oh My Zsh, and searching on the official repo https://github.com/microsoft/vscode I don't see them doing anything like that either. I also didn't see it in the `code` command I have installed under Ubuntu 22.04 with version `1.92.0-insider` of VS Code.

It's possible that you have some alias or function for `code`, for which you could try `which -a code`, or in any case inspect the script that it is running with `cat "${commands[code]}"`.

If it turns out to be a VS Code issue, then gather the information necessary and submit an issue in the upstream repo: https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/new/choose.

body:
You can take a trace of the cd process by running `set -xv` beforehand:
```sh
set -xv; cd <directory>
```
and you should hopefully see at which point zsh stops or hangs running a command. If there is no specific point where it hangs, you should at least see what runs when the directory change happens.

You can then turn off tracing with `set +xv`.

author: carlosala

body:
Most of the appeal of big popular plugins is the system of aliases they define, such as the git plugin, which allow for standardized plugins across many different setups, for example if your colleagues also  use Oh My Zsh, or you deploy those on servers or whatever.

We know that's not for everybody, which is why we added the option of turning off aliases (with zstyle, which allows for quite a big flexibility).

Every plugin has its own README file, as you'll see, and yarn specifically doesn't have much appeal other than aliases.

- Perhaps additional instructions along the lines that you pointed out earlier would be of great aid in locating and modifying a few aliases?  The locate DB is dead to us, and which or where _la_ returns a rather useless: _la: aliased to ls -AFG_
- Is writing a new alias file into the Custom directory the best way to override an alias or three, or would editing them in the lib/file be a better way to go?  If so, is there a tool and methodology in place or does one just jump in  with a text editor?
author: OldSubSailor

body:
If you search `alias ll` on GitHub search will most likely do the trick.
In order to modify the file, I recommend you copy the same structure in the custom directory. That is, a file in `$ZSH/custom/lib/directories.zsh` with modifications will be loaded instead of the default if it exists.
author: carlosala

body: You can also override a single alias by putting it in your .zshrc file, anywhere after the `source $ZSH/oh-my-zsh.sh`. The last defined alias will be preserved and the previous one overriden.

author: OldSubSailor

body:
Oh I see, thanks for spotting that! We must have added it this way to keep the `.zshrc` file as lean as possible.

The files the message is referencing should be immediate children of the custom folder, with `.zsh` extension, such as `$ZSH_CUSTOM/aliases.zsh`.

These files are last to be read (see [^1]), just before loading the theme, so any new changes in there take precedence.

I think we can make the explanation clearer, I'll try to get to it later today.

Thanks again!

[^1]: https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/blob/0c9b42a863778d67aee22bbd43f296110fe1f047/oh-my-zsh.sh#L212

body:
@2art 

Yes, you're allowed to use Python for parts of your plugin. There's no strict requirement that everything must be written in native Zsh. However, keep in mind that using Python will introduce an external dependency, so document it and ensure it's available on target systems. Integrate your Python scripts cleanly with your Zsh code by calling them as needed, while providing fallbacks or error messages if Python isn’t installed.

author: roeniss

body:
By design Oh My Zsh only sources plugins that live within its directory or in the custom folder under `$ZSH_CUSTOM/plugins`.

In the end, a plugin is just a script file that can be sourced, so IMO it is more straightforward to use the `source /usr/share/zsh/plugins` method. But you can also create a symlink in the `$ZSH_CUSTOM/plugins` folder that points to the systemwide folder. Check out https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/wiki/Customization for more info about the custom folder.

author: mcornella

body:
You should provide more information to diagnose this. With the info provided, I understand this:

1. When you type, what you typed gets merged with the prompt.
2. You don't have the `lla` alias available.

I'd say (1) is probably caused by using a non-UTF8 locale, so zsh can't accurately tell how many characters have been written to the terminal. You can check this out by running `locale`, you should have one that ends in `.utf8` or `.UTF-8`. See https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/wiki/FAQ#how-do-i-change-my-locale.

(2) might happen if your current zshrc file does not contain the aliases that you wanted, either because it's badly copied or something is commented out, or any other possibility. There should be a line in there saying `alias lla=...` and no lines saying `unalias lla`.

Without more info it's impossible to confirm any of that. If this doesn't help, please post your redacted .zshrc file.
author: mcornella

body:
Likely your new` .zshrc` (from the other system) references aliases or plugins that don’t exist on this machine. Try these steps:

1. Check that `source $ZSH/oh-my-zsh.sh` is still in your `~/.zshrc.`
2. Inspect `plugins=(...)` in your `.zshrc` to ensure all plugins are installed/available.
3. Verify your `$PATH` (run `echo $PATH`) includes standard bin folders.
4. Restart the shell (`exec zsh`) to reload. If a command still fails, see if it’s defined by an alias or plugin that’s missing.

body: Indeed, it is expected. AND has higher precedence than OR (like multiplication with sum). And, therefore, first gets executed `[[ true ]]`, then the first print (whose block returns `false`) and then the last block, executed because the first block returns falsy.

You can restore the old behavior by customizing Terminal's key bindings. Open Terminal > Preferences > Profiles > Keyboard and modify the key mapping so that Cmd-C and Cmd-V perform the Copy/Paste actions without needing Shift. If necessary, reset to defaults and then reassign the shortcuts you prefer. This should re-enable copying text from anywhere in the line directly with Cmd-C.

author: roeniss

body:
A common reason for “freezes” when tab-completing Git commands in Git Bash + Oh My Zsh is that Zsh is scanning a large directory tree for completions. It may not truly be frozen, just slow.

Try these steps:

1. Disable or limit Git completions temporarily:

```
# In ~/.zshrc
plugins=()  # Remove "git" if present
```
Then open a new shell and see if tab completion still lags.

2. Use a minimal Zsh session to confirm Oh My Zsh is the culprit:

```
zsh -f
# Then manually enable completion:
autoload -U compinit && compinit
```
Test `git diff /path/to/<TAB>` again. If it’s faster, something in your Oh My Zsh config or plugins is slowing it down.

3. Enable visual feedback:
```
# In ~/.zshrc
COMPLETION_WAITING_DOTS="true"
```
This shows dots while Zsh is generating completions, helping distinguish a real freeze from a time-consuming directory scan.

4. Narrow completion scope for Git: You can set Git completion to only show tracked files, ignoring untracked. For instance:
`zstyle ':completion:*:*:git:*' ignored-patterns '*~'`
or other patterns to skip large sets of files.

**If these changes resolve your issue, then you know it was large, slow Git completions in combination with your environment. If it still “freezes,” make sure Git Bash can handle forward slashes and that your Windows path translations aren’t interfering with completion.**
author: KianAnbarestani

body: Look to see if you have a file called `.zshrc.zwc` in your home directory. If it's there, I can probably help you to reconstruct your `.zshrc` from it.
author: agkozak

body:
If `~/.zshrc.zwc` exists, try

    fpath+=( $HOME )
    autoload -Uz .zshrc
    autoload +X .zshrc
    which .zshrc | sed $'1d; $d; s/^\t//;' > $HOME/my_restored_zshrc

Take a look at `~/my restored_zshrc`, and see if it's more or less what you had in your old `.zshrc`. If it's acceptable, you can rename that file to `.zshrc` and use it.

Source: https://www.reddit.com/r/zsh/comments/984j0d/deleted_zshrc_file_but_configuration_did_not_reset/

author: carlosala

body:
Hi @Hyffer, thanks for the thorough post.

After investigation, this ended up being a combined bug in the Tailscale completion setup and our initialization.

- The `Tailscale` command is capitalized in the macOS app (you were on the right path).
- When first loading the plugin, we assign `${aliases[tailscale]:t}` to the `_comps` array. This is equivalent to doing `compdef _tailscale ${aliases[tailscale]:t}`, and the parameter expansion becomes `Tailscale` (capitalized).
- When completing the alias, zsh sees that the command is capitalized, which matches the completion we added and runs `_tailscale`.
- The `_tailscale` completion file is generated via `tailscale completion zsh`.

So far so good.

- On the next shell start, the completion file (`_tailscale`) is already created.
- This file is created with the following header:
  ```sh
  #compdef tailscale
  ```
- When `compinit` runs, it automatically sees the completion file named `_tailscale`, and the `#compdef` header, and therefore does the equivalent of `_comps[tailscale]=_tailscale` (not capitalized).
- After that, since the alias points to the command capitalized, it doesn't match, and we have the problem.

## Fix

We can fix this by either:

1. Have Tailscale generate the completion with both forms:
   ```sh
   #compdef tailscale Tailscale
   ```
   This is difficult because I've seen they usa go's cobra library and I think they can't change it that much.

2. Have Tailscale change the executable to lowercase (`/Applications/Tailscale.app/Contents/MacOS/tailscale`).

In the meantime, there's the 3rd option, which is:

3. Make sure the aliased command is also bound to the completion. This is what I've done in 501f29f90c67bfb47506715c894e710c46ae3e0e. So it should now work out of the box when updating.

Thanks for the report!

body:
What we call `themes` and what terminal emulators call `themes` are different things. Themes in Ghostty are like color schemes (i.e. what shade of red is "red", what shade of green is "green", etc.).

In Oh My Zsh, a theme is what information you see in your prompt, and with which "standard color" ("red"/"green"/"blue"/"yellow"/etc.), you get what I mean. See https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/wiki/FAQ#what-is-a-zsh-theme.

If you just want to have the same color shades, then you can use Ghostty and select the `adventure` theme, which will change the color scheme.

----

If not, and you already knew this information, then what you are asking is to put similar colors on the prompt segments, as how Ghostty shows the prompt:

![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/56e6b583-739f-4987-9cfc-09663b77435c)

The jonathan theme is quite different from the default Ghostty prompt, therefore you'd have to understand which general color is being shown, and modify the theme as a custom theme, as described in https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/wiki/Customization#overriding-and-adding-themes.

This is significantly harder so I don't think you are asking about this, but rather the first one.

body:
You can get the current branch with the `git_current_branch` function, so in your example:

```zsh
# delete the remote branch
git push origin :$(git_current_branch)
```

These two aliases already exist in $ZSH/plugins/git/git.plugin.zsh

```zsh
# gpod = git push origin delete
alias gpod='git push origin --delete'
# git push current origin branch
alias ggpush='git push origin "$(git_current_branch)"'
```

author: rose-munaretto

body:
Within the `PROMPT` variable or other PROMPT variables (PS1 to PS4 and RPS1 to RPS4), the sequences in "SIMPLE PROMPT ESCAPES" (also documented in [PROMPT EXPANSION](https://zsh.sourceforge.io/Doc/Release/Prompt-Expansion.html)) work out of the box.

As soon as you move to zsh code, as you do within a zsh function, you have to resort to other expansion (most commonly parameter expansion and [other special parameters](https://zsh.sourceforge.io/Doc/Release/Parameters.html#Parameters-Set-By-The-Shell)), so you have to find the equivalent. `%!` is the history event number, and in this case you'd be looking at `$HISTCMD`.

The other alternative you used also works, which is forcing prompt expansion on the prompt sequence in a variable. A shorter, uglier version is `${(%):-%!}`, which also uses another parameter expansion trick.

The zsh docs also have other tricks like that, might be useful in the future: https://zsh.sourceforge.io/Doc/Release/zsh_toc.html#SEC_Contents.

body: Hi! `CA` is included as a global alias in the [`common-aliases`](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/blob/db32c6ccce91e0b36873c8bfccd1e40f452a2060/plugins/common-aliases/common-aliases.plugin.zsh#L32). This allows to use it in any place of the command, not only at the start (this would be a regular alias). If `CA` bothers you, you can add a `unalias CA` right after sourcing `oh-my-zsh.sh`, or disable `common-aliases` if that's what you want.

body:
You could create your own function, and add it to your `zshrc`. E.g:
```zsh
function lat {
  if [[ -f $1 ]]; then
    cat $@
  else
    ls $@
  fi
}
```

In this case it defaults to `ls`, unless you pass as a first argument a file.
You can tweak it to match your needs.

body:
This is a known behavior that can sometimes be tweaked by adjusting your Zsh completion settings. You might try adding this to your ~/.zshrc:
`zstyle ':completion:*' completion_auto_quote true`
This setting instructs Zsh to automatically add closing quotes when completing arguments. If that doesn't work, review your Powerlevel10k and Oh My Zsh configurations for any conflicting settings that might override the auto-quoting behavior.
author: KianAnbarestani

body:
Yes, `zstyle ':completion:*' completion_auto_quote true` somewhat improve the problem.

Now, zsh at least preserves opening double quote, single quote, raw string syntax, and even dollar single quoted literal strings, when completing the rest of file path CLI arguments.

But I'd love an option to specify which of the various string syntaxes to apply on TAB. So that I don't have to constantly remind zsh to use the preferred syntax.

... an even more advanced tab completion configuration logic would allow users to declare custom TAB behaviors like _Disregard the opening string syntax. Always prefer raw, unquoted strings unless the given characters (e.g. ASCII spaces) are present in the current tab result, in which case convert to double quotes_.

Some users may prefer single quotes for safety. I use those within POSIX family shell scripts, but prefer double in the live shell REPL, as such commands tend to behave more portably, in terms of publication code snippets and makefile wrappers, so as to not break native Windows shells.

body: FWIW, my terminal ends up in the same state after using `omz reload`. so this is definitely something to do with how these `omz` commands are executed.
author: ZaLiTHkA

body:
This happens because `omz update` recreates the shell session, i.e. it runs `exec zsh` afterwards to apply the changes.

How this looks like to Warp is as if a subshell has been run, so you need to "warpify" the shell again: https://docs.warp.dev/features/warpify/subshells.

TL;DR, it happens the same as if you run `zsh`. The link shows how you can "enable" warp in such cases.
author: mcornella

body: interesting.. haven't seen anything about "warpifying a subshell" before now. I added the `printf` command from that page to my `.zshrc` file now and tried both `omz update` and `omz reload` again, and it does seem to work around the issue... but it does not look all that "pretty": I get the same output with `omz reload` as well. so... thanks, but no thanks. I'll just stick to running OMZ's `upgrade.sh` script directly instead then.

author: roeniss

body:
**@Murdock135 @roeniss** 
Oh My Zsh does not store your PATH or environment variables by default — that’s left to your shell config (usually ~/.zshrc). If you want a clean separation, you can create a separate dotfile (e.g. ~/.zshenv) or place environment settings in ~/.oh-my-zsh/custom/ as a script, which Oh My Zsh will source. Either way, it’s just standard shell practice; Oh My Zsh itself doesn’t manage those variables directly.

* reset the omz repo with `cd ~/.oh-my-zsh && git stash` (or just make a snapshot with copy and backup)
* update omz to the latest version with `omz update`
* try `git config --list` after disabling all the plugins

you said you only enabled those three plugins, but I think you somehow enable `git-commit` plugin ([readme](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/blob/master/plugins/git-commit/README.md#L9-L10)) with something like `plugins=(git-commit)` because "oh-my-zsh.git-commit-alias" is in the plugin: https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/blob/master/plugins/git-commit/git-commit.plugin.zsh#L2-L5

body:
I think I found the cause. Can you check your `~/.gitconfig`?

```
[oh-my-zsh]
	git-commit-alias = "..."
[alias]
	build = "!a() ..."
	chore = "!a() ..."
	ci = "!a() ..."
	docs = "!a() ..."
	feat = "!a() ..."
	fix = "!a() ..."
	perf = "!a() ..."
	refactor = "!a() ..."
	rev = "!a() ..."
	style = "!a() ..."
	test = "!a() ..."
	wip = "!a() ..."
```

If you found these lines in your gitconfig, blow them out. Share the result please
author: roeniss

body:
##### If it works, let me explain what's going on here.

* You may have enabled `git-commit` once before, either intentionally or by mistake.
* `git-commit` plugin modify your git global commit
* With [this line](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/blob/master/plugins/git-commit/git-commit.plugin.zsh#L5), it adds `oh-my-zsh.git-commit-alias` field to config so that the following action only happens once.
* With [this line](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/blob/master/plugins/git-commit/git-commit.plugin.zsh#L57), which is in the loop, it creates multiple git aliases
* Those aliases empower you some useful commit shortcuts e.g., `git feat Add another feature`
* That's why you get those weird line when you execute `git config --list`

##### Side note: what is the "!a()" form?
- It's [git config alias form](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-config#EXAMPLES:~:text=If%20the%20alias%20expansion%20is%20prefixed%20with%20an%20exclamation%20point%2C%20it%20will%20be%20treated%20as%20a%20shell%20command).
author: roeniss

Hi @KMM-G, I'm not sure if you understand my word or not, so I'll add another solution for you. 

Execute this command in your shell. This will delete unnecessary in your gitconfig! :D

```sh
git config --global --unset oh-my-zsh.git-commit-alias
git config --global --unset alias.build
git config --global --unset alias.chore
git config --global --unset alias.ci
git config --global --unset alias.docs
git config --global --unset alias.feat
git config --global --unset alias.fix
git config --global --unset alias.perf
git config --global --unset alias.refactor
git config --global --unset alias.rev
git config --global --unset alias.style
git config --global --unset alias.test
git config --global --unset alias.wip
```

and then check the result:

```sh
git config --list
```
author: roeniss

git config --global --unset alias.test

body:
If you misunderstood my word, that's not your problem. It's my problem. I'm also non-native English speaker 😜 sorry for my word.

Although your issue [seems to be resolved](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/discussions/13022#discussioncomment-12584331) already, I'll add few words about why your `alias` don't show the problem:

Firstly, `alias` is added like this:

```
alias gcmsg="git commit --message"
```

Some plugins use this technique. See [this line](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/blob/3151c9c1a330cdea66dd7d1a24810fe805298711/plugins/git/git.plugin.zsh#L191).

Secondly, Some use another technique.

```
git config --global alias.wip $PREDEFINED_WIP_FUNCTION
```

`git-commit` uses this method. See [this line](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/blob/70395a64637ad1a94b11f6007f29ee0c0d716884/plugins/git-commit/git-commit.plugin.zsh#L57)

**So your `alias | grep git | grep commit` doesn't work as expected. It doesn't exist in aliases but in ~/.gitconfig.**

You need to list both keys in one zstyle directive, not two separate ones. Try this:
`zstyle :omz:plugins:ssh-agent identities id_ed25519 id_ed25519_cc`
Remove separate path entries—oh-my-zsh assumes your keys are in ~/.ssh. Also, ensure file permissions are correct. This should cause both identities to be loaded when a new terminal starts.

body:
See: https://github.com/nasso/omzsh/issues/1#issuecomment-2696983896

> Add this to your `.zshrc` (before loading plugins):
>
> ```zsh
> zstyle ':omz:alpha:lib:git' async-prompt no
> ```

This workaround should probably be added to the README...
author: nasso

body:
it does indeed work, thanks!

What does it do exactly? disable async handler for the git prompt? then should I maybe also remove any async in my theme?
author: victornoel

body:
my understanding is:
- by default `git_prompt_{status,info}` does something similar to what `_prompt_{status,info}_async` does in your theme
- `_prompt_{status,info}` are being registered as asynchronous handlers and any code inside is already being run asynchronously (including calls to `git_prompt_{status,info}`)
- for some reason, "nesting" asynchronous operations like that doesn't work
- `zstyle ':omz:alpha:lib:git' async-prompt no` tells `git_prompt_{status,info}` to always operate synchronously
- because `_prompt_{status,info}` are asynchronous already, the call to the now synchronous `git_prompt_{status,info}` won't block/slow down the prompt!

the whole point of doing these asynchronous things is to allow the prompt to be shown immediately while the slower things are being computed in the background, and then update the prompt once they are ready. that's why you don't need `git_prompt_{status,info}` to do its own asynchronous magic: you're already doing that in the theme (so that `jj` doesn't slow down your prompt). `zstyle ':omz:alpha:lib:git' async-prompt no` tells `git_prompt_{status,info}` "don't worry about being slow, just print your final output".

so if you call other things inside of `_prompt_{status,info}`, make sure they operate synchronously. alternatively (and depending on what you want to show in your prompt), you may create a different function (outside of `_prompt_{status,info}`) and directly call it from `PROMPT="..."`. then, it can have its own async handler without clashing with `_prompt_{status,info}`.

i hope that makes things more clear!
